segment-text: "THE JOLSON STORY"
Adaptation by Harry Chandlee & Andrew Solt
They roll up, sepia-tinted, as Jolson sings "LET ME SING AND I'M HAPPY."
EXT. WASHINGTON, D.C. STREET - DAY
A quaint turn-of-the-century street with horse-drawn carriages and the Capitol building in b.g. SUPER: "WASHINGTON, D.C. at the turn of the century"
EXT. KERNAN'S THEATER - DAY

The marquee reads: "KERNAN'S BURLESQUE." Below it hangs a sign: "GORDON & WHITE'S PARISIAN NIGHTS: 20 BEAUTIFUL GIRLS." We hear the sound of the orchestra inside playing an upbeat number. TILT DOWN to the busy sidewalk.
INT. KERNAN'S - THE STAGE - HIGH ANGLE FROM THE BALCONY

Six girls sit on the stage, legs outstretched, in golden skirts as a seventh, with one leg raised to her shoulder, dances on one foot in front of them. CLOSER ANGLE FROM THE BALCONY The girls behind her do a tumble and a traveler comes down. The audience applauds. STILL CLOSER - ANGLE OVER THE ORCHESTRA The orchestra plays a final chord, then the traveler rises again. PAN left to where the spotlight waits.
(calling) Hey, over here!
THE STAGE - HIGH ANGLE FROM THE BALCONY
The spotlight swings to the right to illuminate STEVE MARTIN, a crusty Vaudeville veteran in a tuxedo and top hat. He holds a viola and plays a note. MEDIUM SHOT - STEVE
Steve Martin's the name.
He plays the note again.
REVERSE ANGLE - THE BALCONY

The patrons laugh. Sitting in the second row are ASA YOELSON and ANN MURRAY, each about 13 years old and childhood friends. THE STAGE Steve plays and dances to a Russian song, kicking his feet out in front of him and doing tumbles. WIDER ANGLE - OVER THE ORCHESTRA He does one last tumble, rises to his feet and throws his arms up. His left hand reaches offstage, then returns holding a cello instead of a viola. He does a double-take and points to it with his bow. It grew!
The audience laughs again. Steve walks to center-stage. A chair slides out from the wings, stopping right behind him. Thank you!
sits in the chair.
Tell ya what I'll do. You call out a tune and I'll play it.
He looks around. WIDER ANGLE ON THE STAGE A man's voice calls out from the audience:
"Stay in Your Own Backyard!"
It's too dirty back there!
The audience laughs. Steve raises his hat, revealing that his bangs are sewn into it. He puts the hat back on. REVERSE ANGLE - OVER STEVE AT THE AUDIENCE
Any other tunes?
A MAN rises.
"Banks of the Wabash!"
Steve begins to point at him with his right hand. ONSTAGE Steve points at the man with the forefinger of his left hand, which also holds the bow.
"Banks of the Wabash." All right. Now I'll tell ya what I'll do. I'll play it if you folks'll sing it. Right? (gestures to the conductor) The key of "C," Professor.

He puts his bow to the cello and plays a low "C." Then he starts to play "BANKS OF THE WABASH" with the orchestra, but no one sings. He looks around disconcerted, taps the bow against the stage, stopping the orchestra, and hangs an arm over his cello.
So you won't sing, heh? Am I asking you to do me a favor? (points the bow at
I'm doing you a favor! When you sing, what happens? Your lungs get full of oxygen. It puts roses on your cheeks!
REVERSE ANGLE - OVER STEVE AT THE AUDIENCE
As a matter of fact, you either sing out or you get out!
He points to the exit. The audience laughs. FRONT ANGLE ON STEVE
Now that we understand each other... (points his bow at the conductor) Let's go, professor.
He starts to play again. The orchestra joins in but, again, no one in the audience sings, except...
IN THE BALCONY - ASA AND ANN
Asa starts to hum the song, then he sings it. ONSTAGE Steve, amazed at the voice he's hearing, stops playing, rests his bow against the side of his cello, and looks up at the balcony, just listening.
IN THE BALCONY
Asa finishes the song. The audience applauds exuberantly. Steve applauds with them. IN THE BALCONY Asa looks around, impressed at the reaction. ONSTAGE
Fine! Fine! Stand up and let the folks see ya!
Asa shakes his head, too shy for accolades.
Ah, don't be bashful! Come on, stand right up!
Steve waves his hands in the air, goading the audience to make
Come on, folks. stand up.
Let's make him
The audience applauds loudly. IN THE BALCONY Asa rises to his feet.
ANGLE OVER STEVE AT THE AUDIENCE
Let's have it again! (points his bow at Asa) Give that boy a spotlight!
IN THE BALCONY
A spotlight lands on Asa. He squints up at the glare.
Professor, let's have the chorus again!
The orchestra plays the intro to "BANKS OF THE WABASH." ONSTAGE Steve, sitting in his chair, waves his bow around as if conducting the intro.
IN THE BALCONY

Asa sings the chorus again. Ann looks at him adoringly. He finishes with his arms outstretched. The audience erupts in applause. Steve applauds enthusiastically. IN THE BALCONY Asa, almost embarrassed, sits down again. pats his shoulder. A PATRON behind him INTERCUT - ASA IN BALCONY/STEVE ON STAGE
What's your name? Asa Yoelson.
Are you in show business? (looks at Ann and smiles shyly) No, sir, I'm not.
Where'd you learn to sing like that? Well, I sing with my father at the synagogue.
At the synago-(catches himself) The synagogue!
He suddenly looks at Ann in a panic.
MEDIUM SHOT - THE BALCONY
Asa grabs Ann's hand and they run up the steps. WIDER ANGLE - OVER STEVE AT BALCONY He calls to them helplessly as they keep running...
Hey, wait a minute! (jumps to his feet) Sonny, wait a minute!
EXT. WASHINGTON STREET - DAY
Asa runs up a sidewalk holding his cap, Ann running behind him.
ANGLE ON THE SYNAGOGUE
Asa reaches the front door but stops, hearing his father singing inside. He runs to a back door.
INT. SYNAGOGUE - LONG SHOT - SAME TIME

Asa's father, CANTOR MOSES YOELSON (known as "Papa"), leads a boy chorus in a Hebrew song. CLOSER ON THE CHORUS Asa enters through a back door wearing a prayer shawl and yarmulke and takes his place among the other boys.
INTERCUT - THE YOELSON FAMILY DURING THE SONG

- MEDIUM CLOSE-UP: Papa looks at Asa sternly. - ANGLE ON ASA (OVER PAPA'S SHOULDER): harmony with his father. Asa starts singing - IN THE GALLERY: MRS. YOELSON (known as "Mama") looks on. She looks classically beautiful in a formal dress. She glances down at her prayer book. - Asa continues singing harmony. - Papa continues singing, conducting the others. - Asa continues singing harmony. - ANGLE OVER ASA AT PAPA: Papa sings the final phrase. - ANGLE OVER PAPA AT ASA: Asa harmonizes the ending with him. He sings the final note.
INT. YOELSON APARTMENT - LIVING ROOM - DAY
Asa sits on the arm of a chair. Answer me, Asa. that time? Papa and Mama look on.
Where were you all
CLOSER ON PAPA
You came like someone who was running a race. It is not good to run a race with God, Asa.
Asa remains sitting on the arm of the chair as Mama looks at him. TWO-SHOT - ASA AND MAMA
Where were you, Asa? I was... singing.
PAN on him as he walks over to Asa and Mama.
You mean you stayed home, practicing? Is that what you mean? No.
Then where were you singing? -- Stop cracking your knuckles.
In the street maybe? That's not exactly where I singing. Singing prayers in the street! Well, no, not exactly.
Exactly? Asa, you must have been exactly somewhere, singing exactly something, and for some exact reason. Yes, Papa.
The doorbell rings. the door. They all look over. Mama goes to answer ANGLE ON THE DOOR Mama opens the door. hand. Steve enters, holding his hat in his
(to Mama) My name is Martin and I... (looks over and sees Asa) ... I'd like to talk to you for a minute.
Asa looks up at Papa and back at Steve, panicked. PAN on Steve as he walks over to Asa. Mama follows him.
Hello, Asa. (extends his hand to Papa) You're Cantor Yoelson. I'm Steve Martin.
(shaking his hand) How do you do?
I'm glad to know ya. real voice.
Your son has a
Oh, you were at the synagogue today for the service.
Uh, me? Uh, no. I just went by there to find out where you live.
Well, then where did you hear my son sing? At Kernan's. Kernan's?
MAMA
Yeah, the burlesque house a few blocks from here.
The burlesque theater!
Don't you ever go there? No, I do not!
They all look at Asa.

That boy's got a future in show business. You see, I've been looking for something like him for a long time. I'd like to make him a regular part of the act. You've got nothing to worry about. I'm booked solid for the season and I move on to Baltimore tonight. Baltimore!
Oh, I know he's pretty young, but when a boy's got a voice like that, he ought to let people hear it.
You're right, Mister Martin. Asa will sing, but where his people have always sung.

I know how you feel, Mister Yoelson. It's a beautiful thing to sing in church. But there's a lot to say for the theater, too. And if you're worried about my taking good care of him, why, I -PAPA Excuse me, Mister Martin. I think I know what's best for my son.
(looks to Mama then Papa) I was afraid that's how you would feel about it. (to Asa) Sorry Asa. (to Mama and Papa) Good night. Good night.
PAN on Steve as he walks to the door and lets himself out. THREE-SHOT - PAPA, MAMA & ASA Papa walks up behind Asa.
(he holds Asa's arms from behind) ... I want your promise that you'll never go to that place again. Asa cannot, does not, say anything.
I can't.
Mama walks up to him.
I -- I can't promise.
Go to your room.
Asa walks off.
INT. ASA'S BEDROOM - NIGHT

He paces the floor. door. Suddenly he stops and looks back at the He turns to his dresser, takes some items from a drawer and puts them in his pockets. Then he grabs a black cap and goes to the window. ANGLE OVER ASA'S SHOULDER - ANN'S WINDOW Asa does a three-note whistle. Ann walks to her window across from him; she wears pajamas and holds a book. She smiles at him. INTERCUT - ASA AND ANN
Ann! Go downstairs and get a ladder! Put in here! Why?
Hurry up! Get the ladder!
Ann, puzzled, turns away, then Asa turns away putting on his cap.
EXT. WASHINGTON RAILROAD YARD - NIGHT
Asa, wearing his cap, runs along the tracks. building looms in b.g.
The Capitol
Asa runs across some tracks and jumps onto the side of a moving freight train. PAN on the train as it moves off, to reveal a rounded sign that reads "WASHINGTON YARD LIMITS."
EXT. BALTIMORE RAILROAD YARD - DAY

Angle on a rectangular sign that says "BALTIMORE YARD LIMITS." PAN over to the train as it comes to a halt. to the outside of a railroad car. Asa still clings He jumps down and brushes himself off. DOLLY IN as a RAILROAD GUARD walks up to him from behind. The guard grabs his arm and walks him away.
EXT. A SIGN - DAY
The sign reads: "ST. MARY'S HOME FOR BOYS."
INT. ST. MARY'S - CORRIDOR - DAY

A priest (JOHN) walks down the hall. Following him are an Irish policeman with a thick accent (REILLY) and Asa, whom Reilly holds by the arm. Asa holds his cap in his hands. PAN on them as they pass a statue of Jesus.
What did you bring me here for? Why don't you let me find Steve Martin?
(sniffing) Shush! What kind of car was that you jumped out of?
that make? What difference does
All the difference in the world, my boy.
INT. FATHER McGEE'S OFFICE - DAY - SAME TIME
FATHER McGEE stands waiting for them, hands behind his back. Reilly escorts Asa into the room; John stands by the door.
Here's another one, father.
Reilly takes his hat off, still holding Asa's arm, as John closes the door.
Yes, let him go, Reilly. (Reilly obliges) Don't mind him. He just thinks he has to behave like a policeman. (to Reilly) And I don't think he understands boys. Oh, no? meself.
I've only got seven of 'em
That's just what I mean. (touches Asa's elbow) Now, suppose you tell me about yourself.
Asa sits in a chair.
Where are you from?
Asa looks down, saying nothing.
Where was he picked up at?
In the freight yard. He came in on a cattle car. Can't ya tell?
McGee "tisks" at Asa.
If you knew anything about freight trains, you'd never pick a cattle car. I had to get here. To Baltimore?
Why?
He's got a job here.
What kind of job? Singing. Singing!
Where? In a show with Steve Martin. in Baltimore right now!
What about your family? know about this?
He's Do they Asa looks down again, not answering. McGee, with John beside him, looks down at Asa.
I see. You know, before we do anything else, I think you'd better have a hot bath. (turning to John) And something to eat, John.
Come on, my boy.
Asa rises and faces McGee.
Why don't you find Steve Martin and ask him?
(taking Asa's elbow) That's just what I'll do.
McGee turns Asa toward John. DOLLY IN on them.
(continuing) And since it's singing he's interested in, John, why not let him sing?
Asa turns back in surprise.
INT. CHAPEL - DAY

CLOSE ANGLE on Asa standing amidst a boy's choir. He wears a pink striped shirt, holds a lyric sheet and looks around uncomfortably, listening to the others sing "AVE MARIA" in Latin. Finally he joins in. DOLLY BACK to show the entire choir.
INT. MCGEE'S OFFICE - SAME TIME
McGee stands lighting a cigar as Steve sits across from him. The boy choir is heard in b.g.
Tell me a little more about your work in the theater, Mister Martin.
I've told you about all there is. (rising) Pardon me, father. I don't get it. Why would you be bringing me here, asking me all these questions? Is there something you want me to do?
I'll come to the point in a little while, Mister Martin.
IN THE CORRIDOR - SAME TIME
Reilly leads Mama and Papa around a corner and down the hall. Papa wears a black hat. He sees the statue of Jesus. Mama gestures to him. Papa hesitantly removes his hat.
INT. McGEE'S OFFICE - SAME TIME
McGee and Steve stand waiting. door and Mama and Papa enter. From outside, Reilly opens the
(politely) Cantor Yoelson? I'm Father McGee.
They shake hands. Reilly exits, closing the door behind him. How do you do?
McGee turns to Mama and shakes her hand. Misses Yoelson.
(looking over McGee's shoulder) Hello. Glad to see ya again.
Oh, you know Mister Martin? We know him.
Is our boy here? The description is like Asa.
I have a very strong feeling that... The sound of Asa's voice now comes through singing above all the others. He sings in Latin. So have I!

McGee leads them to the door to the chapel. CLOSER ANGLE McGee opens the door. DOLLY IN behind them as they enter the chapel. The choir, in front of them, continues singing. THEIR POV - THE CHOIR Asa, in the center of the group, keeps singing. FOUR-SHOT - MAMA, PAPA, STEVE AND McGEE Mama steps forward, concerned. Asa!
MAMA
THEIR POV - THE CHOIR keeps singing. TWO-SHOT - MAMA AND PAPA Mama keeps looking as Papa looks over her shoulder.
(shaking his head)
Singing without his cap on! TWO-SHOT - STEVE AND McGEE McGee gives Papa a kindly look.
It's not so much what's on the head as what's in the heart, is it, cantor?
Papa gives McGee a kindly look. CLOSE-UP - ASA He sings "amen," looking idly around. on something -- his parents. Suddenly his eyes land
FOUR-SHOT
Mama, Papa, McGee and Steve look on as Asa and the choir sing the penultimate "amen."
ANGLE ON THE CHOIR
The other boys sing the final "amen." The CHOIR DIRECTOR looks back. McGee signals to Asa. Asa steps forward. THE FOURSOME Asa approaches them.
Hello, Mama. Hello, Papa. Beautiful song, wasn't it?
They all walk into McGee's office. DOLLY IN after them.
INT. McGEE'S OFFICE - SAME TIME
DOLLY BACK as Asa stops at McGee's desk and sets the lyric sheet down. McGee closes the door. Get ready, Asa.
We're going home.
(turning to face him) I'll do it again, Papa. I'll run away. I'll find Mister Martin someplace.
Steve holds his hat out.
Now wait, Asa...
MEDIUM CLOSE SHOT - STEVE His hat is at his side again.
Don't ya understand? I wouldn't have you in the act if I didn't have your father's consent. Asa persists.
Then I'll get in another act. keep running away.
What are we going to do with him? We'll decide that at home.
(facing Papa) Maybe we should decide now, Papa. (to Steve) You said you would take very good care of our boy?
Asa looks on in amazement.
You would consider this foolishness?
I'm only asking, Papa. If Asa is going to keep running away, I have a right to ask.
I made inquiries about Mister Martin, and he's a man who can be trusted.
Oh, I'm sorry I started all this trouble, cantor. And I wouldn't have anything more to do with it -except considering how Asa feels about show business -Show business!
(to Steve) Tell me, Mister Martin. what would Asa do?
The same as I did at Kernan's, Mama. Isn't that what you mean, Mister Martin? Yes.
Asa sits in a chair.
waits for somebody else to begin.
Asa moves into a chair. McGee, Steve, Mama and Papa look on.
... I'm up in the balcony, and Mister Martin says, "Come on, folks! Join in the chorus!"
Steve sits in the chair opposite him and gestures upwards.
Don't be bashful! All together now!
And then, all of a sudden, I stand up in the balcony and start singing!
I'm dumbfounded at the kid's voice and I say...
INT. THEATER BALCONY - NIGHT
Asa stands in a gray suit with a gray hat in his hands. spotlight moves onto him.
... give that boy a spotlight!
Asa sings "WHEN YOU WERE SWEET SIXTEEN."

He sits in a chair onstage, playing his cello while Asa sings, then leans back and folds his arms contentedly. When Asa finishes, the audience applauds vigorously. smiles back at the people behind him in the balcony.
INT. TRAIN COMPARTMENT - MOVING - DAY
Asa, wearing a gray suit and cap, writes on the back of a postcard. Steve sits stoically beside him, arms folded. CLOSE SHOT - THE POSTCARD It reads: "Dear Mama and Papa, Got lots of applause. Next
Then Pittsburgh. Feeling fine, Your loving son, Asa Yoelson."
INT. YOELSON LIVING ROOM - DAY
Mama reads the postcard aloud as Papa stands, watching and smoking a cigarette, and Ann lingers in b.g.
Next week, Philadelphia, then Pittsburgh. Feeling fine. Your loving son, Asa Yoelson. (looks at Papa) He's feeling fine.
Mama turns to a bulletin board. Nothing is posted on it. DOLLY IN as Papa and Ann follow her. onto the board. Mama pins the postcard
Philadelphia, Pittsburgh. Washington was to small for Asa.
is now covered with postcards. DOLLY IN on a postcard that says, "VIEW NORTH ON MAIN STREET."
MONTAGE - ASA'S POSTCARDS
PAN across them as Asa, his face SUPERIMPOSED, sings "AFTER THE BALL." Mama and Papa comment on each card in turn: - The first postcard is labeled, "Indianapolis."
Indians! yet! MAMA (O.S.)
He'll come home scalped,
- The next reads, "Kickabock, Iowa."
Don't be foolish!
- The next reads, "Dubuque, Iowa."
Oh, no. That's pronounced "DIHBik," Mama. Dih-BYOOK!
MAMA (O.S.) Anyway, in Doo-boo-kay, he sang two encores.
INT. TRAIN COMPARTMENT - MOVING - DAY
Asa and Steve sit facing each other in train seats. holds a book; Asa holds a pencil and note-pad.
Now spell, uh, "tedious." T-E-D-O-...
Wait a minute. That's wrong. again. "Tedious."
You wouldn't know how to spell it if you didn't have that book in front of you!
(glowering back) I'm not learning, I'm teaching!
INT. THEATER - BALCONY - NIGHT

Asa, in a dark gray jacket and tie, sings "BY THE LIGHT OF THE SILVERY MOON," clapping his hands together on the beats. When he gets to the word "moon," he sings it "moo-hoo-hoon." ONSTAGE - STEVE is dancing while he holds his cello and twirls his bow on his finger. He suddenly does a double-take. Asa resumes singing normally and Steve regains his composure and starts dancing and twirling his bow again.
IN THE BALCONY

When Asa gets to the line "We'll be cuddling soon," he adds a jazzy "uh-huh." ONSTAGE - STEVE sitting in his chair now and playing the cello, hits a sour note. He looks up at the balcony nonplussed. IN THE BALCONY Asa finishes the song. to them happily. The audience applauds. Asa nods back ONSTAGE Steve looks at him glowering.
BACKSTAGE - A CORRIDOR

A very grumpy Steve comes down from the wings as dancing girls head onstage. At the same time, Asa comes down a staircase from the balcony holding a black cap. As Asa passes Steve, Steve taps his shoulder with the bow. Just a minute!
DOLLY BACK as they walk down some steps and along the corridor.
How come, all of a sudden, in the middle of the song you put in a
"moo-moo?"
Oh. You thought it would be a little better. If the guy who wrote the song wanted to say "moo-moo," he'd write it that way.
I just get tired of singing it the
same way every time, Mister Martin, so -They arrive at their dressing room door. the bow. Steve gestures with
So you thought up "moo-moo." I didn't think it. out.
It just came
It just came out. (holding his bow out angrily) Well don't let it come out! You sing that song just like it's written and I don't want to hear no more "moo-moos." Oh.
Steve goes into the dressing room.
How do you like that!
IN THE DRESSING ROOM - STEVE
sets his cello against a wall.
He gets tired singing it the same way, so he puts in... (makes a face, rolls his eyes and snaps his fingers) ... "moo, moo." As if that makes any difference!
He crosses past Asa to the door and hangs up his coat.
And another thing. You're singing it a little faster every night. (into Asa's face) Is that because you're tired, too?
No, sir. That's because you're playing it too slow.
Steve, taking off the hat with fake bangs, suddenly freezes.

I'm playing it slow? Excuse me! I've only been in this business for thirty years -- making a very nice living. But maybe an old trooper like you oughta come down on the stage and have people -ASA Oh, I've thought about that. I mean, I could sing much better on the stage.
You don't tell me!
Yes, because then I could sing right to 'em instead of the backs of their heads, and I could see how their faces looked. Faces?
Unless you can see the people's faces, it's no good at all.
You don't say. And tell me, Mister Yoelson, with that spotlight shining right in your eyes, how ya gonna see faces?!
Well, that's easy. If you turn up all the lights in the theater, then they can see me and I can see them.
Steve starts to mull it over, then does another double-take and glares at Asa again.
INT. YOELSON LIVING ROOM - DAY
Mama holds a postcard, looking at it excitedly.
Papa rises, smoking a cigarette, and looks over her shoulder. THEIR POV - THE POSTCARD It pictures Asa wearing a checkered suit and trousers and holding a hat with his arm on a chair.
INT. THEATER - BALCONY - NIGHT
Asa, wearing the same suit, sings "GOODBYE MY BLUE BELL." People behind him look on smiling. ONSTAGE Steve dances, twirling his bow.
IN THE BALCONY

Suddenly Asa tries to hit a high note and his voice breaks. ONSTAGE Steve stops dancing and looks at him. IN THE BALCONY Asa looks back at the spectators, who laugh at him now. ONSTAGE Steve beckons anxiously to him to continue.
IN THE BALCONY
Asa tries again to sing but barely croaks the words out. holds his throat. More laughter from the crowd.
He

In a panic, he looks behind him, then suddenly he puts his fingers into his mouth and starts to whistle -- a whistle as sharp and true as a musical instrument. ONSTAGE Steve looks up in awe, rises smiling and points his bow at Asa.
Asa continues whistling as Steve dances merrily again. At the end of the song, both Steve and the audience applaud wildly. Asa runs from the balcony.
INT. DRESSING ROOM
A dejected Asa sits in a chair with Steve looking on.
You don't want to be in the act any more?
(tears in his eyes) No, Steve. I'm goin' home.

Ah, don't be silly. Your voice is changing, that's all. In a couple of years you'll get it back, better than ever. (leans over to him) And listen. The way you pulled that whistle out of the hat, that's showmanship! Didn't you hear them
But it isn't the same.
What isn't the same? The act's just as good as it was before! When you whistle, their faces don't look the same.

(throwing his arms up) Yah, faces again! (pacing) This kid'll drive me crazy! (kneels beside Asa) Listen. I didn't say you're gonna whistle the rest of your life. Just for a while. Look. (holds Asa's shoulders) You know what we're gonna do? We're gonna work up some whistling routines and you're gonna work on the stage with me! What do ya think of that?
Asa looks at him like a ray of sunshine.
And do ya know something else? Then you're gonna get billing! Yes, sir! Meet that scintillating team of artists, Steve Martin and Asa Yoelson! (he rises, thinking) Asa Yoelson.
He strokes his chin.
INT. YOELSON LIVING ROOM
Mama walks through the room reading another postcard aloud.
Now I whistle in the act. Everyone
seems to like it. Your loving son, Ah -She stops, puzzled, and looks more closely at the card. she calls out. Papa!
Asa isn't Asa any more! What!?
Hurrying to her side, Papa takes the postcard and looks at it. It is signed "Al Jolson of Martin and Jolson." Al Jolson?!
MONTAGE - MORE OF ASA'S POSTCARDS

DOLLY IN on the bulletin board, now covered with postcards. DISSOLVE TO a PAN across several cards as Asa, superimposed, whistles and Mama and Papa comment as before. - The first postcard depicts "Reno, Nevada."
In Reno, Papa, he's picking up time.
Is time something you can pick up? This is crazy!
- The next postcard reads: "Walla Walla." As we see it, Asa's image dissolves into an image of the adult Jolson.
Walla-Walla. This town they liked so much they named it twice! - The next postcard reads: "Los Angeles, California."
He says he can't come home this summer, Papa. Maybe next spring.
The superimposed Jolson stops whistling and smiles.
INT. YOELSON LIVING ROOM - DAY
Mama, flanked by Papa and Ann, holds a postcard and smiles at it. Papa shakes his head in admiration. THE POSTCARD shows the adult Jolson posing with Steve. image of Jolson. DOLLY IN on the
INT. HOTEL ROOM - CLOSE-UP OF JOLSON - NIGHT
He lies on his back in bed, hands behind his head, and hums a few bars of "WHEN YOU WERE SWEET SIXTEEN." The next line he sings:
... since first I met you on the village green...
He stops singing and smiles, impressed with his voice. WIDER ANGLE Jolson throws back the covers, jumps up, hurries over to Steve's bed and slaps his bottom. Steve!
Listen to this!
Jolson sits on the edge of Steve's bed.
(awakening) What's the matter? What's the matter?
Listen to this! (resumes singing) Come to me, for my dream of love is all, I love you as I loved you, when you were sweet, when you were sweet (speaking) What do you think?
Are we going into this again? the middle of the night?
But it's getting better every time, Steve!
All right, all right. It's getting better. I told you, let it alone. Give yourself time. Go away. Go sing in the bathroom.
Why not let me try?
You mean, all of a sudden, come on maybe, in the middle of the act, you want to bust out singing? Why not? You're daffy! Why?
JOLSON
We ain't changin' no act in the middle of the season and fall flat on our face! We're gonna do like I said. We're gonna knock off a month next spring!
Steve snuggles against his pillow.
Never stops!
PAN on Jolson as he walks back to his bed.
This guy'll drive me crazy!
It reads "CHURCHILL DOWNS, LOUISVILLE, KENTUCKY."
He's getting close to home! see him soon, Papa!
INT. THEATER - BACKSTAGE - NIGHT
Jolson and Steve walk in through the back door and turn to an OLD MAN behind a desk. Hello, Pop.
How's tricks?
(standing) Letter for you!
He hands Jolson a letter from a cubby-hole.
